description: How a generated web mini-app should add SOUND EFFECTS: reuse the built-in, dependency-free AudioManager Lego (pure WebAudio synthesis, zero .mp3/.wav, offline-safe under file://) instead of hand-rolling a bespoke oscillator setup from scratch. 2D games use resources/AudioManager.js; 3D games use web-components-3d/effects/audio-manager-3d.js. Music toys (synth / drum machine / sequencer) are a DIFFERENT path: vendored Tone.js (see tone-js pack), never AudioManager.

guidelines.rule: For one-shot game SFX in a 2D web mini-app, REUSE the built-in AudioManager Lego rather than synthesizing audio from scratch. Import the named export and call the high-level dispatcher: `import { AudioManager } from './web-components/resources/AudioManager.js'; const audio = new AudioManager({ masterVolume: 0.05 }); audio.sfx('score'|'hit'|'gameover'|'levelup'|'click'|'powerup');` An unknown name falls back to a soft default tone. Low-level escape hatches exist if you need a specific sound: `audio.beep({freq,duration,type:'square'|'sine'|'triangle'|'sawtooth',slideTo,volume})` and `audio.noise({duration,volume})`.
guidelines.why: AudioManager is a maintained, offline, zero-asset Lego. Rolling a bespoke `new (window.AudioContext)()` + oscillator graph from scratch duplicates it, tends to be inconsistent across events, and is exactly the kind of reinvention the catalog exists to prevent. The real, working API is sfx()/beep()/noise()  -  NOT the phantom `playEffect()`/`resume()` that an older _index.json contract advertised.
examples: GOOD: audio.sfx('hit') on collision, audio.sfx('levelup') on level clear, audio.sfx('gameover') on death.
BAD: hand-writing `const ctx = new AudioContext(); const osc = ctx.createOscillator(); ...` inline for every event when AudioManager.sfx() already does it.
GOOD (custom): audio.beep({ freq: 880, duration: 0.08, type: 'square', slideTo: 220 }) for a bespoke zap.

guidelines.rule: For a 3D (three.js / WebGL) game, use the 3D audio Lego instead of the 2D one: `import { AudioManager3D } from './web-components-3d/effects/audio-manager-3d.js'; const audio = new AudioManager3D({ camera }); audio.attachTo(loop); audio.play('shoot'|'hit'|'explosion'|'pickup'|'coin'|'levelup'|'jump'|'footstep'|'ui'|'hurt'|'powerup');` Pass `{ position: obj.position }` to spatialize (needs camera + THREE). `attachTo(loop)` reuses the game loop's first-tap unlock, so never block the render loop on audio.
guidelines.why: AudioManager3D is procedural ADSR synthesis with optional THREE.PositionalAudio panning  -  the right primitive for a 3D scene. The 2D AudioManager works too but misses spatialization and the loop-unlock integration.
examples: GOOD: audio.play('footstep') on movement tick; audio.play('hit', { position: enemy.position }) for a spatialized impact.
GOOD: audio.attachTo(gameLoop) once at setup so the first tap unlocks the AudioContext.

guidelines.rule: Construct the audio manager at load (cheap  -  it does NOT open an AudioContext yet), but DEFER the first sfx()/play() call until AFTER a user gesture (tap / key / Start button). iOS Safari and in-app WKWebViews boot the AudioContext suspended and require a gesture before the first resume().
guidelines.why: Calling sfx() before any gesture silently no-ops on iOS and can log AudioContext warnings. Both AudioManager classes resume() lazily and no-op safely until the first gesture, so wire the first sound to game-start/first-input.
examples: GOOD: play the first sound on the Start-button click or first arrow-key press, not on page load.

guidelines.rule: Pair 2D sound with the built-in mute control: `import { MuteToggle } from './web-components/ui/MuteToggle.js'; MuteToggle.mount(document.body, { audio });` It renders a small top-right pill and persists the mute state to localStorage. (3D: use audio.setMuted()/toggleMuted() wired to your own button; the game stays fully playable while muted.)
guidelines.why: Players expect a mute affordance; MuteToggle is a one-liner and persists across sessions so you don't re-implement it.
examples: GOOD: MuteToggle.mount(document.body, { audio }) after constructing the AudioManager.

guidelines.rule: If `./web-components/resources/AudioManager.js` (or the 3D equivalent) is NOT present in the working directory (common on an ITERATE of an existing single-file app, where the Lego kit isn't seeded), do NOT abandon the pattern and do NOT add a network/CDN dependency. Either (a) copy a compact AudioManager class body INLINE into the app's <script> and keep calling the same `sfx('hit'|'levelup'|'gameover'|...)` convention, or (b) write a tiny self-contained WebAudio helper that mirrors that same named-sfx interface. Stay OFFLINE and self-contained either way.
guidelines.why: The iterate/branch workdir is often just the app's own files with no Lego kit. The goal is still to converge on the AudioManager conventions (named sfx dispatcher, gesture-gated, mute-able, offline WebAudio) so future extraction is trivial  -  not to justify a from-scratch one-off that ignores the house style.
examples: GOOD (iterate, no Lego on disk): inline a ~40-line AudioManager with sfx()/setMuted() and call audio.sfx('gameover').
BAD: give up and pull howler.js or a .mp3 pack from a CDN because AudioManager.js wasn't in the folder.

guidelines.rule: SFX and MUSIC are different Lego. AudioManager is for one-shot game sound effects ONLY. For a musical instrument, synth, drum machine, step sequencer, or FFT/audio visualizer, use vendored Tone.js (loaded as a classic UMD <script src>, rewritten offline by the WEBLIB B-catch) and the entities/StepSequencer Lego  -  see the tone-js wisdom pack. Never use AudioManager for melodic/sequenced audio, and never load Tone via ESM/importmap (silent under file://).
guidelines.why: Tone.js provides transport/scheduling/instruments AudioManager deliberately lacks; conflating them produces a broken music toy or a needlessly heavy SFX layer.
examples: GOOD (drum machine): vendored Tone.js UMD + entities.step-sequencer.

_last_updated: 2026-07-10  -  created (Phase LB-SND): route 'add sound' asks to the built-in AudioManager Lego instead of from-scratch WebAudio; iterate-workdir inline fallback (se-005).
